title: Pici cacio e pepe
subtitle: Thick Sienese pasta with black pepper and aged pecorino
excerpt: Pici, the thick hand-rolled pasta of the Sienese hills, with coarsely cracked black pepper toasted in a dry pan and emulsified with pasta water and pecorino into a sauce that clings to every strand. No cream. Three ingredients and technique.
sourceNotes: Pici is documented as a hand-rolled pasta of the Sienese hills in regional Italian cooking literature from the nineteenth century, made from flour and water without egg. The cacio e pepe preparation (black pepper and aged sheep's-milk cheese emulsified with pasta water) originates in Roman cooking but is applied to pici throughout Tuscany. Artusi discusses the pasta traditions of central Italy and Tuscan cheese preparations in La scienza in cucina (1891, public domain).

content.text: Pici is the pasta of the Sienese countryside: thick, hand-rolled, made from flour and water without egg. Its weight and starchiness make it an ideal vehicle for cacio e pepe. The fat noodle releases more starch into the cooking water than any dried pasta, giving you a pasta water thick enough to emulsify pecorino into a coherent, clinging sauce without any cream.

The pepper must be cracked coarsely, not ground. Toast it in a dry pan until fragrant before any liquid touches it. Beyond that, the technique is timing: the cheese goes in off the heat, the hot pasta water holds it together, and the pasta is tossed immediately before the sauce sets.

items.name: Dried pasta
items.prepNote: pici, or thick spaghetti or tonnarelli

items.name: Black pepper
items.prepNote: whole peppercorns, coarsely cracked in a mortar

content.text: Crack the {{black-pepper}} of peppercorns coarsely in a mortar. You want shards and pieces, not powder. Tip into a wide frying pan over medium heat, dry, no oil. Toast for 60 to 90 seconds, shaking the pan, until the pepper smells fragrant and slightly smoky. Set the pan aside off the heat.

Bring a large pan of generously salted water to a boil. Add the {{pasta-dried}} of pici. Cook for the time on the packet, checking 2 minutes early, pici varies widely in thickness. Scoop out at least 250 ml of pasta water before draining. Drain.

Return the pepper pan to medium heat. Add 150 ml of the pasta water and let it bubble for 30 seconds to pick up the pepper's heat. Remove from the heat completely. Add the {{pecorino-romano}} of pecorino in three additions, stirring vigorously between each. The water and cheese should form a creamy, pale sauce. Add the drained pici immediately and toss well, adding more pasta water a splash at a time until every strand is coated and the sauce moves loosely. Serve immediately.

items.fix: Remove the pan from the heat completely before adding the cheese; the residual warmth of the water does the work
items.cause: Pan still on heat when cheese was added; too-hot water melts the protein before it emulsifies
items.symptom: Cheese clumps into strings rather than a sauce

items.fix: Add pasta water in small splashes, not all at once. Add the pecorino in three goes. Stir hard after each.
items.cause: Too much pasta water added at once, or cheese not added in stages

items.fix: Reserve at least 250 ml of pasta water; toss the pasta into the sauce immediately after draining, while both are hot
items.cause: Not enough pasta water reserved, or pasta left to sit before tossing
items.symptom: Pasta is dry with barely any sauce

items.fix: Toast cracked peppercorns in a dry pan for 60 to 90 seconds; never use pre-ground pepper for this dish
items.cause: Pepper not toasted, or pre-ground pepper used instead of coarsely cracked whole peppercorns
items.symptom: Pepper tastes sharp and raw rather than warm and aromatic

content.text: Pici is the pasta of the Sienese countryside: Montalcino, Pienza, Montepulciano, and the Crete Senesi. The shape is a very thick, irregular spaghetti, rolled by hand against a wooden board. It is uneven in places, with a dense chew that no machine-extruded pasta quite matches. Putting the Roman cacio e pepe method on a Tuscan pasta is a natural crossover. Both styles prize the same thing. They want starch, fat, and pepper in balance, with nothing else getting in the way. The result is heavier and more satisfying than the Roman original. The thicker noodle carries more starch, so the sauce binds more easily.
